name: Adjective Agreement
summary: Portuguese adjectives change their ending to match the gender and number of the noun.
explanation:
Portuguese adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number. Most adjectives end in -o and have four forms: -o (masc. sg.), -a (fem. sg.), -os (masc. pl.), -as (fem. pl.). So alto (tall) becomes alto, alta, altos, altas.

Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant usually have one form for both genders and only change for plural: inteligente becomes inteligentes; fácil becomes fáceis.

Adjectives normally come after the noun: uma casa branca (a white house), um carro vermelho (a red car).
commonMistakes: Leaving the adjective in its -o dictionary form regardless of the noun.
Putting colour or description before the noun. Portuguese says casa branca, not branca casa.
Forgetting that -e adjectives still change in the plural (grande to grandes).
Making a feminine plural end in -os instead of -as.
Matching the adjective to the English noun rather than the Portuguese one.
tip: Mismatched genders take the masculine plural: o João e a Maria são altos.
